methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: SendCutSend
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
AIM Intelligent Machines is a Series A robotics and manufacturing company that has raised $50M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a broader Robotics & Manufacturing sector comprising one public comparable (277810.KQ) and eight private peers, including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity.

Stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable approach, using the $50M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A advancement to later stages, yielding $100M. A peer-median approach was not feasible: while seven same-sector peers exist, the narrow filters for stage alignment, adjacent-stage comparables, and valuation-tier similarity yielded fewer than three datapoints, making sector-wide aggregation unreliable and potentially misleading.

The triangulated valuation range is $85M to $115M, with a median of $100M, grounded in the single applicable method. Key uncertainties include the appropriateness of the 2.0x multiplier to this company's specific growth trajectory and capital efficiency relative to peers; limited comparables prevent validation against market-equivalent transactions in the robotics space. Any revision should await either narrower peer dataset clarification or additional data on AIM's revenue, unit economics, or customer concentration relative to the five named private peers.
